The reason I didn't do this boolean statement was that the user doesn't know which variable is missing, and the solution is now specific to two arguments. As a general rule, if I have to deal with multiple elements I handle them as n elements and print errors nicely.

This code can later be pulled out into a helper to handle the general case of validating groups of n config items, and we aren't manually querying the underlying sources of configuration in multiple ways with different strings, all of which could be subtly wrong and will require manual replacement in multiple places if refactored.

It's possible to build the docs without having the package installed, so pkg_resources can't be relied upon. Granted the `.. autoXXX` stuff may (?) not work as desired if the package isn't installed, but that is an option, and presumably it's a common enough one that RTD doesn't install the package by default. So I prefer not to have conf.py depend on the package being installed, and an external version.txt is one way to do that while also DRY.

Note that you're enforcing unique `suite.id()`, but the filtering checks `str(suite)`.  These are different:

- for regular unittest.TestCase tests `id()` returns `package.module.ClassName.test_method`, while `str()` returns `test_method (package.module.ClassName)`
- for doctest.DocFileSuite tests, `id()` returns `filename_txt`, while `str()` returns `/absolute/path/to/the/filename.txt`.

(Incidenally, this is why the original bug report doesn't apply to the test runner: we can always distinguish different distribution.txt doctest files if they're in different directories).

Not sure... We used to have `to` and `sender`. Now we have `target`, `sender` and `receiver`.

- `to` and `target` refer to more or less the same thing I guess (the first account that is called)
- `sender` changes meaning: it used to be the one who signed the transaction, now it's the "owner" of the `target` account (and just happens to put a signature in the `data` field)
- `receiver` is the second hop (the account that's called by the `target` account)

So from a technical standpoint, I don't see a lot of overlap between original and new terms. I could imagine renaming `target` to `to` for consistency (although I'd prefer `target` in a vacuum). Renaming `receiver` to `to` could be misleading on the protocol level, but would make sense from a high level point of view in most cases. I don't see a good alternative to `sender`.

From a matching perspective we look for two things: the project (confluent-kafka-python, which is already a top-level directory) and the git ref (tag or sha) which is currently included in the sub-directory.
Multiple CI services may provide artifacts for the same git ref, and even multiple artifacts from the same service (e.g., travis-osx, travis-linux, windows-x64, windows-x86, ..).

It is not possible to conditionally change the deploy path based on whether a tag is present or not, so the best we can do is:
`confluent-kafka-python/<TAG>-<SHA>/<ci>-<variant>...`

And since we might not be confident that we know both the sha and tag at the same time we would still need to enumerate on confluent-kafka-python/*.

We could use just the tag, `confluent-kafka-python/<TAG>/`, but there is no guarantee that it will be unique if the tag is later moved and artifacts rebuilt, which would result in conflicting artifacts under that tag using different SHAs.

This is happening because the object on the right-hand side is a tuple. Strictly, old-style Python string formatting works with `string % tuple` where the elements of the tuple are inserted into the string. Python is seeing *three* things that you want to inject into the string but only seeing *one* `"%s"`. For full safety with this doing `"%s" % (this_index_cache,)` will ensure the correct behaviour, otherwise using `.format()` is the modern recommended method.

2. Wait, so that a task can be assigned. The metric for how long to wait is an interesting question. For example, the test could be dumb and wait one second, or it could be smart but fragile and wait based on the output of `qpid-stat -q`.

Note also that if the sample_size is null, we effectively set it to max_step - min_step + 1 which is the total number of distinct steps.  As it happens that means our formal now has (max_step - min_step) in both numerator and denominator, so they cancel and each step gets assigned a unique bucket index of step - min_step.

If sample_size is *greater* than the total number of steps, the bucket index for each step is assigned as m * step + C where m > 1, so each step will still get a unique bucket index and hence all steps will be selected.

The issue with `all/any` is that when you pass them a tuple, the entire tuple has to be eagerly constructed before they can evaluate it. When they consume a generator, this isn't a problem since they never see the elements that aren't required.

Normally I don't care either way, though I think @lodrion has established that his preference is for `and/or` explicitly rather than `any/all`. But in this case, since we're calling functions, I'd much rather not call those functions if the result can be determined from the first element. This is why the clauses are ordered in rough order of increasing complexity.

From my understanding, this PR is supposed to test the Lambda proxy integration. So we basically need to construct the following request flow:

Client request -> API Gateway -> Lambda function

So, as part of the test we need to create:
* the API Gateway resources
* a simple Lambda function

The test should then assert that the client request is successfully forwarded to the Lambda function. The question is: how we can make that assertion, because we need some kind of "shared state" between the test execution and the Lambda function. There are several possible options, but one of the simplest I can think of is to simply create an S3 bucket with a distinct name from within the Lambda function, and then assert in the test script (`test_api_gateway.py`) that the bucket has actually been created.

If we go with the option of creating the S3 bucket, then it appears that there is no need for a HTTP backend service in this test. Therefore, we can probably remove the `TestListener` class from here, am I right?

This raises a question -- if a node fails to give a name to an output that is computed (optional or not), what is the failure mode? It seems to me that giving a name to an output that is __not__ computed is definitely an error, while the reverse isn't as obviously wrong. I guess that some implementations could have trouble with both.

Giving a name to an output that is not computed: this could happen even with non-optional outputs. When this named output is subsequently used in a different node, it would pretty much behave like the use of an uninitialized variable (the corresponding tensor memory could have random values). (Obviously, this depends on the runtime implementation.) Unfortunately, we cannot always catch this statically.

Failing to give a name for an output that is not optional is easier to handle: model validation can catch this, and a runtime can avoid initiating execution. Even when it can't be caught in model validation, it can be caught at runtime. How this exception is reported to the user is implementation-dependent. I guess ONNX could try to standardize the exception behavior in such cases.
